title: 1Password vs Passbolt for Power users
meta_description: 1Password vs Passbolt for power users who want to host password infrastructure on company servers. See why Passbolt wins with self hosting.
constraint_lens: Power users prefer tools that can run inside their own infrastructure so they control how credential systems are deployed and managed.

one_second_verdict.summary: Best for power users who want password management running on their own company servers.
one_second_verdict.reason: 1Password fails first because it relies on a vendor hosted vault instead of allowing a self hosted deployment.

verdict.summary: Passbolt is the better choice for power users who want to host credential infrastructure internally. It can be deployed on a company server where administrators manage storage, updates, and access control. 1Password stores credentials inside a vendor hosted vault that requires relying on the provider infrastructure. For organizations that want full control of where credential data lives, requiring a hosted vault breaks the requirement.
verdict.decision_rule: If password management requires relying on a vendor-hosted vault instead of a self-hosted deployment, 1Password fails first.

sections.heading: Why Passbolt fits power users
sections.content: The user manages internal infrastructure and wants credential management integrated directly into company servers. Passbolt can be installed on a private server environment controlled by the organization. Administrators manage how the system runs, how credentials are stored, and how access is granted. This allows password management to operate alongside other internal infrastructure.

bullets.point: Passbolt can be deployed on a self hosted server controlled by the organization.
bullets.why_it_matters: Teams can integrate password management directly into internal infrastructure.

bullets.point: Passbolt stores credential data inside the organization's server environment.
bullets.why_it_matters: Administrators control where passwords are stored and how the system operates.

bullets.point: Passbolt allows administrators to manage system configuration and updates on their own servers.
bullets.why_it_matters: Power users can adapt the system to internal infrastructure requirements.

bullets.point: 1Password synchronizes passwords automatically through its hosted vault service.
bullets.why_it_matters: Users can access credentials across devices without deploying infrastructure.

bullets.point: 1Password provides browser extensions that automatically fill login forms.
bullets.why_it_matters: Users can sign into websites quickly without copying credentials.

bullets.point: 1Password maintains the underlying infrastructure and service updates.
bullets.why_it_matters: Organizations avoid maintaining their own password management servers.

items.fails_when: The organization does not want to install or maintain a server for password management.
items.what_to_do_instead: Use 1Password where the password vault is hosted and maintained by the provider.

items.fails_when: The company requires internal infrastructure control because 1Password stores credentials in a vendor hosted vault.
items.what_to_do_instead: Use Passbolt where password management runs on a self hosted server.

sections.heading: When this verdict might flip
sections.content: If the organization prefers a hosted password manager and does not want to operate internal infrastructure for credential management, 1Password may become the better option.

sections.heading: Quick decision rules
rules: Pick Passbolt if you want password infrastructure running on company servers.
Pick Passbolt if internal deployment and server control matter.
Pick 1Password if you want a hosted password manager without managing infrastructure.

faqs.q: Why do power users choose Passbolt?
faqs.a: Passbolt can be deployed on company servers so organizations control their credential infrastructure.
No. 1Password stores credentials inside a vendor hosted vault service.

faqs.q: Can Passbolt manage credentials for teams?
faqs.a: Yes. Passbolt provides permission based access for sharing credentials across teams.
Organizations that prefer a hosted password manager without managing servers may prefer 1Password.
